name: Mexico City

palette.name: National Green
palette.rationale: National flag green is visible in capital ceremonies and public symbolism; it is national, not a city-brand claim.

palette.name: National Red
palette.rationale: National red supports civic-symbol contexts tied to the capital's monuments and ceremonies.

palette.name: Cantera Gold
palette.rationale: Warm stone summarizes historic-center facades, plazas, and late-afternoon monumental light.

palette.name: Chapultepec Green
palette.rationale: A muted park green represents Chapultepec, median planting, and highland urban vegetation.

palette.name: Tezontle Red
palette.rationale: Dark volcanic tezontle and historic masonry add a grounded red-brown architectural layer.

palette.name: Talavera Blue
palette.rationale: A ceramic blue accent references tilework and applied decorative craft visible across central Mexico.

palette.name: Market Marigold
palette.rationale: Orange-gold captures market flowers, street food warmth, and festival color without official status.

palette.name: Metro Asphalt
palette.rationale: A dark infrastructure neutral helps designers represent transit, roads, and dense urban texture.

sources.title: Secretaría de Turismo de la Ciudad de México
sources.publisher: Gobierno de la Ciudad de México
sources.note: Used for official tourism and civic context.

sources.title: Símbolos Patrios
sources.note: Used to distinguish national-symbol colors from editorial city colors.

notes: Complete seed record; official flags refer to Mexican national symbols visible in the capital, while city-specific material colors remain editorial.
editorialSummary: Mexico City's complete palette combines national civic colors with cantera, volcanic stone, parks, ceramic blue, market warmth, and infrastructure neutrals for layered urban design work.
